h1Title: Acute Tonsillitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute tonsillitis is infection and inflammation of the palatine tonsils — paired lymphoid structures at the sides of the oropharynx — producing sore throat, tonsillar enlargement, and systemic symptoms. It is one of the most common infections in childhood, with each child averaging 1-2 episodes per year before age 15. Group A Streptococcus causes 15-30% of bacterial cases; adenovirus and other viruses account for the majority of the remainder. The condition is clinically important because untreated bacterial tonsillitis can progress to peritonsillar abscess, and recurrent episodes burden quality of life substantially. Penicillin for 10 days is first-line treatment for bacterial tonsillitis; tonsillectomy reduces episodes by 50-70% in children with documented recurrent disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Tonsillitis accounts for approximately 1.7 million emergency department visits per year in the United States; over 530,000 tonsillectomies performed annually in the US making it one of the most common surgical procedures in children
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence in children aged 4-12 years; adolescents and young adults account for a second peak; streptococcal tonsillitis is 3-4 times more common in children than adults
keyStats.avgAge: Most common between ages 5-10 years; recurrent tonsillitis meeting surgical criteria peaks in children aged 4-8
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 616 million cases of group A Strep throat globally per year (WHO), with tonsillitis contributing substantially to this burden; peritonsillar abscess complicates approximately 30 per 100,000 person-years

definition: Acute tonsillitis (ICD-10: J03.90) is an acute inflammatory condition predominantly of the palatine tonsils, marked by erythema, swelling, and commonly exudate of the tonsillar crypts, lasting up to 14 days. The palatine tonsils are part of Waldeyer's ring — a circle of lymphoid tissue guarding the upper aerodigestive tract — along with the adenoids (pharyngeal tonsil) and lingual tonsil. Tonsillitis is distinguished from pharyngitis by the dominant involvement of tonsillar tissue rather than the posterior pharyngeal wall, though in clinical practice the two conditions frequently coexist as tonsillopharyngitis. Pathophysiologically, viruses or bacteria colonize tonsillar crypts, triggering a local inflammatory response with exudate formation, lymphocyte recruitment, and cytokine-mediated fever. Bacterial tonsillitis is caused most commonly by Group A Streptococcus (S. pyogenes), but Group C and G streptococci, Fusobacterium necrophorum, Staphylococcus aureus, Haemophilus influenzae, and anaerobes also contribute. Acute tonsillitis is managed by primary care physicians, pediatricians, and ENT surgeons; the latter provide surgical management when episodes meet frequency thresholds or when complications arise.

typesClassification.description: Adenovirus (most common cause overall), rhinovirus, coronavirus, Epstein-Barr virus, and enterovirus. Epstein-Barr virus causes infectious mononucleosis with severe bilateral tonsillar enlargement, grey-white exudate, and posterior cervical lymphadenopathy — clinically distinct from GABHS tonsillitis and should not be treated with aminopenicillins.

Group A beta-hemolytic Streptococcus (S. pyogenes) causes 15-30% of tonsillitis in children and 5-15% in adults. Distinguished clinically by sudden onset, fever, tonsillar exudate, and absence of cough; confirmed by rapid antigen test or throat culture. Requires antibiotic treatment to prevent rheumatic fever.

Group C and G streptococci, Fusobacterium necrophorum, and mixed anaerobes also cause acute tonsillitis. Fusobacterium is associated with Lemierre's syndrome (jugular vein thrombophlebitis). These organisms do not cause rheumatic fever but may cause suppurative complications.

Defined as 7+ episodes in one year, 5+ per year for two consecutive years, or 3+ per year for three consecutive years (Paradise criteria). Indicates persistent reservoir of pathogen in tonsillar crypts or impaired local immunity. Meets criteria for tonsillectomy consideration per AAO-HNS guidelines.

Complication of tonsillitis where infection extends into the peritonsillar space. Cellulitis (without discrete pus collection) may resolve with antibiotics alone; abscess requires needle aspiration or incision and drainage. Presents as unilateral tonsillar/peritonsillar swelling, uvular deviation, and trismus.

primarySymptoms: Severe sore throat with odynophagia — pain on swallowing that may be severe enough to prevent oral intake, particularly in children who refuse food and fluids.
Bilateral tonsillar erythema and enlargement, often graded 1-4 based on degree of airway encroachment — grade 3-4 (tonsils touching midline) can cause snoring, sleep-disordered breathing, or stridor.
Tonsillar exudate — white, yellow, or grey follicular or confluent pus on the tonsillar surface, present in 50-70% of bacterial tonsillitis and also in EBV mononucleosis.
Fever above 38°C in the majority of bacterial cases; lower-grade fever in viral tonsillitis; high fever (above 39°C) combined with exudate and lymphadenopathy strongly suggests bacterial aetiology.
Tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy — palpable, sometimes visible swollen lymph nodes in the front of the neck, present in 70-90% of GABHS cases.
Halitosis (bad breath) from tonsillar exudate, dead cell debris in crypts, and bacterial metabolites — a particularly distressing symptom for school-age children.
Muffled or 'hot-potato' voice quality when tonsillar swelling encroaches on the oropharyngeal airway or in peritonsillar abscess.
Referred ear pain (otalgia) on swallowing via the glossopharyngeal nerve, experienced by up to 50% of patients with significant tonsillitis.
Drooling in young children unable to swallow saliva because of odynophagia — also a red flag for peritonsillar abscess or epiglottitis.
Headache, myalgia, and malaise proportional to the degree of systemic inflammatory response.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden onset severe sore throat with high fever (above 38.5°C) and absence of rhinorrhea or cough — classic GABHS presentation requiring rapid antigen testing.
Unilateral tonsillar swelling greater than the contralateral side, with ipsilateral soft palate edema — early peritonsillar cellulitis that may progress to abscess.
Difficulty swallowing saliva or drooling in a child with fever — suggests severe tonsillitis or early peritonsillar abscess formation.
Progressive fatigue, posterior neck lymphadenopathy, and bilateral tonsillar exudate in an adolescent — suggests EBV infectious mononucleosis.
Tonsillar enlargement causing snoring, gasping, or observed pauses in breathing during sleep — suggests sleep-disordered breathing from adenotonsillar hypertrophy.
emergencySigns: Trismus (inability to fully open the mouth), severe unilateral tonsillar bulge with uvular deviation, drooling, and muffled voice — peritonsillar abscess requiring emergency ENT drainage.
Stridor, respiratory distress, or oxygen desaturation with massive bilateral tonsillar enlargement — impending airway obstruction, a life-threatening emergency.
High fever with neck stiffness and bulging in the lateral neck or posterior pharyngeal wall — retropharyngeal or parapharyngeal abscess requiring urgent CT and surgical drainage.
Signs of systemic sepsis (tachycardia, hypotension, altered consciousness) with tonsillitis — bacteraemia requiring immediate IV antibiotics and emergency department assessment.
Sore throat with sudden onset of neck swelling, high fever, and new respiratory symptoms in a young adult — suspect Lemierre's syndrome (fusobacterial septic jugular thrombophlebitis).

causes.description: The dominant bacterial cause of acute tonsillitis, responsible for 15-30% of cases in children. Transmitted via respiratory droplets; incubation period 2-5 days. Produces streptolysins, toxins, and M protein that evade host immunity. Untreated infection carries risk of suppurative complications (peritonsillar abscess) and non-suppurative sequelae (rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis).

Adenovirus is the most common single cause of tonsillitis across all ages, producing follicular tonsillitis that can closely mimic bacterial disease. Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) causes infectious mononucleosis with bilateral exudative tonsillitis, posterior lymphadenopathy, and splenomegaly. Rhinovirus and coronavirus cause milder tonsillar involvement as part of upper respiratory tract infection.

Fusobacterium necrophorum causes peritonsillar abscess and Lemierre's syndrome — internal jugular vein thrombophlebitis with septic pulmonary emboli — in young adults. Mixed anaerobic flora from tonsillar crypts contributes to recurrent and chronic tonsillitis biofilm formation, driving persistent symptoms.

causes.cause: Group C and G Streptococcal infection
causes.description: Non-group A streptococci cause a clinical syndrome identical to GABHS tonsillitis but rapid antigen tests return negative. They are more common in adults. Though rheumatic fever risk is not established, suppurative complications including peritonsillar abscess can occur.

causes.cause: Staphylococcus aureus and Haemophilus influenzae co-infection
causes.description: Beta-lactamase-producing Staphylococcus aureus and H. influenzae are commonly isolated from recurrent tonsillitis and peritonsillar abscess cultures. They may be responsible for penicillin treatment failures by inactivating penicillin within the tonsillar tissue, necessitating amoxicillin-clavulanate or clindamycin.

riskFactors.description: School-age children are the primary risk group for acute bacterial tonsillitis because of immature adaptive immunity, frequent GABHS exposure in classroom settings, and high tonsillar lymphoid activity. The attack rate falls substantially after age 15 as partial immunity develops.

riskFactors.factor: Exposure to infected individuals in classroom or household
riskFactors.description: GABHS spreads efficiently via respiratory droplets. Household contacts of a GABHS-positive child have a 25-30% secondary attack rate. Classrooms sustain transmission chains particularly in winter and spring.

riskFactors.factor: History of prior tonsillitis or GABHS carriage
riskFactors.description: Patients who have had three or more prior episodes have a significantly higher risk of recurrent infection. GABHS carriers (culture-positive without symptoms) can reintroduce bacteria into households and trigger recurrent symptomatic infections.

Common variable immunodeficiency, IgA deficiency, HIV, and systemic corticosteroid use predispose to recurrent and severe tonsillitis with atypical organisms. Tonsillitis refractory to appropriate antibiotics should prompt immunological evaluation.

Crowded housing, dormitories, and military barracks increase transmission of streptococcal and viral pathogens. GABHS outbreaks in closed communities (military recruits, boarding schools) are well documented.
GABHS tonsillitis peaks from October to April, corresponding with school terms and cold-weather indoor congregation. Adenoviral tonsillitis has additional summer peaks in some regions.

Children in smoking households have higher rates of recurrent tonsillitis and upper respiratory infections compared with non-exposed children, likely through impaired mucosal immunity and disrupted mucociliary clearance.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 4-12 years are most frequently affected; tonsillectomy is the most common elective surgical procedure in US children, with over 530,000 performed annually.
GABHS tonsillitis is 3-4 times more common in children than adults; adults still account for a significant portion of strep cases and peritonsillar abscess.
Infectious mononucleosis tonsillitis peaks in adolescents and young adults aged 15-25; it is uncommon in children under 10 and adults over 30.
Fusobacterial tonsillitis and Lemierre's syndrome disproportionately affect otherwise healthy young adults aged 16-30 and are an underrecognized cause of severe pharyngotonsillitis in this group.
diagnosisOverview: Acute tonsillitis is diagnosed clinically by tonsillar erythema, enlargement, and often exudate on direct examination of the oropharynx, combined with systemic symptoms of fever, lymphadenopathy, and sore throat. The key clinical question is whether GABHS is present, guiding antibiotic decisions. The Centor/McIsaac score stratifies this probability from examination findings — exudate, anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, fever, absence of cough — and age. A score of 2-3 warrants rapid antigen detection testing (RADT); a score of 0-1 makes GABHS unlikely. RADT has sensitivity of 70-90% and specificity above 95%; negative RADT in children should be confirmed by throat culture given rheumatic fever risk, per IDSA 2012. In adults, negative RADT alone is sufficient to withhold antibiotics. When infectious mononucleosis is clinically suspected (adolescent, bilateral tonsillar enlargement with posterior lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, profound fatigue), monospot testing is performed. Monospot is less sensitive in children under 12 and in the first week of illness — VCA IgM serology confirms EBV in these cases. CT of the neck is reserved for suspected peritonsillar abscess, retropharyngeal abscess, or parapharyngeal space infection. Throat culture specifically for GABHS remains the gold standard with 90-95% sensitivity and can also grow non-GABHS streptococci, fusobacterium, and other pathogens relevant to treatment decisions in refractory cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Clinical stratification of GABHS probability to guide RADT and antibiotic prescribing
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician asks you to open wide and say 'ah'; a tongue depressor is used to visualize the tonsils. Neck lymph nodes are palpated. The entire examination takes under 5 minutes. Score 0-1: no testing needed; 2-3: RADT; 4+: consider empirical antibiotics or RADT.

diagnosticTests.test: Rapid antigen detection test (RADT) for GABHS
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid bedside confirmation of group A Strep tonsillitis to guide antibiotic prescribing within minutes
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A dry swab is firmly pressed on each tonsil and the posterior wall for 5-10 seconds — briefly uncomfortable. A lateral-flow immunoassay returns a result in 5-10 minutes. Sensitivity 70-90%; specificity above 95%.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for GABHS confirmation; recommended when RADT is negative in children given rheumatic fever risk
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same swab technique as RADT. The specimen is incubated on blood agar at 37°C for 18-24 hours. Results returned next business day. Sensitivity 90-95% for GABHS; also grows other pathogens relevant to surgical cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for infectious mononucleosis (EBV) in adolescents with exudative tonsillitis and posterior lymphadenopathy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A finger-prick or venous blood sample. The test detects non-specific heterophile antibodies. Result in 5 minutes. Sensitivity 85-90% in adolescents; low in children under 12 and in the first week of illness.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and EBV antibody panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: EBV mononucleosis confirmation; also assesses leukocytosis, lymphocytosis, and atypical lymphocytes typical of EBV
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood draw. Leukocytosis with >10% atypical lymphocytes is characteristic of EBV. VCA IgM, EA IgG, and EBNA IgG profile indicates stage of EBV infection. Results in 1-5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies peritonsillar, retropharyngeal, or parapharyngeal abscess; assesses airway compromise from massive tonsillar enlargement
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: IV contrast is injected. The scan takes 5-10 minutes. Results available within 1-2 hours in the emergency setting. Abscess appears as a rim-enhancing fluid collection adjacent to the tonsil or posterior pharyngeal wall.

treatmentOverview: Management of acute tonsillitis is stratified by aetiology. Viral tonsillitis, which accounts for the majority of cases, is managed supportively with analgesics, hydration, and rest — antibiotics provide no benefit. For confirmed or clinically suspected GABHS tonsillitis, antibiotic therapy is indicated to prevent acute rheumatic fever, shorten illness duration by 1-2 days, reduce spread, and decrease risk of peritonsillar abscess. Penicillin V (500 mg twice daily for 10 days in adults; 250 mg twice daily in children) or amoxicillin (50 mg/kg once daily for 10 days) is first-line per IDSA 2012. For penicillin-allergic patients, cefalexin (first-generation cephalosporin) is preferred for non-anaphylactic allergy; clindamycin or a macrolide is used for anaphylactic penicillin allergy, though macrolide resistance in GABHS exceeds 5-10% in many communities. Recurrent tonsillitis not responding to penicillin may involve beta-lactamase-producing co-pathogens (S. aureus, H. influenzae); amoxicillin-clavulanate or clindamycin is preferred in these cases. Adjunctive therapies including a single dose of corticosteroid (dexamethasone 10 mg in adults; 0.6 mg/kg in children) reduce throat pain duration by approximately one day. NSAIDs (ibuprofen) are superior to paracetamol for pharyngotonsillitis pain. Peritonsillar abscess requires needle aspiration or incision and drainage under local anesthesia plus antibiotic therapy. Tonsillectomy is the definitive intervention for recurrent acute tonsillitis meeting Paradise criteria (7+ episodes per year, 5+ per year for 2 years, or 3+ per year for 3 years, all documented with fever or exudate or positive strep test) and is also performed for tonsillar hypertrophy causing obstructive sleep apnea.

medicalTreatments.name: Penicillin V (500 mg orally twice daily for 10 days; 250 mg twice daily in children)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antibiotic for GABHS tonsillitis per IDSA 2012. Narrow spectrum, well tolerated, and universally active against GABHS — no penicillin resistance has been documented. Twice-daily dosing improves adherence over three-times-daily regimens.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces GABHS pharyngeal carriage to under 5% by day 10; prevents acute rheumatic fever in over 99% of cases; reduces symptom duration by 1-2 days vs placebo

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin (50 mg/kg once daily up to 1 g, or 500 mg twice daily for 10 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative to penicillin V; more palatable liquid suspension for children. Available as once-daily dosing which improves adherence. Do not use if EBV mononucleosis is suspected due to drug-rash interaction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent GABHS eradication to penicillin V; once-daily dosing achieves 85-90% adherence vs 65-70% for three-times-daily regimens in pediatric trials

medicalTreatments.name: Ibuprofen (400 mg every 6-8 hours in adults; weight-based in children)
medicalTreatments.description: First choice analgesic and anti-inflammatory for tonsillitis pain. Superior to paracetamol for throat pain relief in head-to-head RCTs. Take with food to reduce gastric irritation. Use for the first 3-5 days or until fever resolves.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces pain intensity by 30-45% at 2 hours compared with placebo; superior to paracetamol by 0.5-1 point on an 11-point pain scale in RCTs

medicalTreatments.name: Single-dose dexamethasone (10 mg orally or IM for adults; 0.6 mg/kg for children)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunctive corticosteroid for moderate-to-severe tonsillitis with significant odynophagia. Reduces time to meaningful pain relief and complete symptom resolution. Given alongside antibiotics in bacterial tonsillitis or alongside analgesics in severe viral disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete pain relief 7.2 hours earlier than placebo in systematic review; NNT approximately 3-5 for pain relief at 24 hours

medicalTreatments.name: Clindamycin (300 mg three times daily for 10 days in adults; 8 mg/kg/day in children)
medicalTreatments.description: Preferred antibiotic for penicillin-anaphylactic allergy or recurrent GABHS tonsillitis unresponsive to penicillin. Covers GABHS, anaerobes including Fusobacterium, and beta-lactamase-producing pathogens. Also used for GABHS carriers with persistent positive cultures.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: GABHS eradication rate 90-95% in treatment failures after penicillin; active against anaerobes and beta-lactamase producers implicated in recurrent disease

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (875/125 mg twice daily for 10 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for recurrent tonsillitis where beta-lactamase-producing co-pathogens are suspected, or where multiple penicillin courses have failed. Broader coverage than plain amoxicillin.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Eradication of GABHS in 88-93% of penicillin-failure cases in comparative trials; also eliminates co-infecting H. influenzae and S. aureus

surgicalOptions.name: Tonsillectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the palatine tonsils under general anesthesia. Indicated for recurrent tonsillitis meeting Paradise criteria, obstructive tonsillar hypertrophy causing sleep-disordered breathing, peritonsillar abscess (second episode or first episode in select high-risk patients), and unilateral tonsillar asymmetry with malignancy concern. Techniques include cold dissection, electrocautery, coblation, and harmonic scalpel; outcomes are similar across methods.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces tonsillitis episodes by 50-70% in the year after surgery compared with watchful waiting in RCTs; primary hemorrhage risk approximately 0.5%, secondary hemorrhage (post-operative days 5-10) approximately 2-3%

surgicalOptions.name: Needle aspiration or incision and drainage of peritonsillar abscess
surgicalOptions.description: Performed by ENT under local anesthesia in the emergency or outpatient setting. Needle aspiration has 90-95% success for uncomplicated abscess and is equivalent to formal incision and drainage with less patient discomfort. Repeat aspiration is needed in 10-15% of cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution of abscess in 90-95% after one to two aspirations plus antibiotics; approximately 10-20% recur and require tonsillectomy within 1-2 years

alternativeTreatments.name: Honey in warm water or tea
alternativeTreatments.description: Modest evidence from small RCTs supports honey for reducing pharyngotonsillitis discomfort; anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Safe in adults and children over 12 months. Not disease-modifying but safe adjunct.

alternativeTreatments.name: Salt water gargling
alternativeTreatments.description: Gargling with 0.9% saline (one teaspoon in 250 mL warm water) four times daily reduces mucosal edema and provides temporary pain relief. Widely recommended in guidelines as a safe, low-cost adjunct.

whySeeSpecialist: ENT referral is warranted when tonsillitis episodes meet Paradise criteria for tonsillectomy, when peritonsillar abscess occurs, when tonsillar asymmetry raises malignancy concern, or when tonsillar hypertrophy is causing sleep-disordered breathing or failure to thrive in children. Infectious disease consultation is appropriate for refractory tonsillitis, suspected EBV complications (splenic rupture, airway compromise), or immunosuppressed patients.
doctorSelectionGuide: A primary care physician or pediatrician manages most acute tonsillitis. Seek an ENT for surgical evaluation, abscess drainage, or complex cases. When choosing a tonsillectomy surgeon, look for someone who performs high-volume pediatric tonsillectomies and uses intraoperative monitoring for obstructive cases. For very young children (under 3), a pediatric ENT with specific experience in small-airway cases is preferred.
hospitalCriteria: Peritonsillar abscess with airway concern, inability to drain in clinic, or severe trismus preventing adequate examination
Inability to maintain oral hydration due to severe odynophagia — IV fluids and pain management required
Retropharyngeal or parapharyngeal abscess on CT imaging requiring surgical drainage under general anesthesia
Systemic sepsis or signs of bacteraemia requiring IV antibiotics
Infectious mononucleosis with airway compromise from massive bilateral tonsillar enlargement or splenic rupture
keyFacilities: Primary care clinics with RADT capability for same-day strep testing
ENT outpatient clinics for abscess drainage and surgical evaluation
Hospitals with ENT on call for emergency peritonsillar abscess drainage
Pediatric surgical centers experienced in tonsillectomy under general anesthesia
Sleep medicine clinics for children with snoring and sleep-disordered breathing from adenotonsillar hypertrophy
preventionStrategies: Wash hands frequently during respiratory illness season and teach children to wash hands after school and before eating.
Keep GABHS-infected children home from school or day care for at least 24 hours after starting antibiotics and until fever-free.
Replace toothbrushes after completing a strep tonsillitis course to remove bacterial colonization from bristles.
Annual influenza vaccination reduces influenza-related upper respiratory infections that predispose to secondary bacterial tonsillitis.
Address passive smoke exposure — children in smoking households have measurably higher rates of recurrent tonsillitis and upper respiratory infections.
For children with 5+ episodes in two consecutive years, early ENT referral for tonsillectomy evaluation reduces overall antibiotic use and missed school days.
lifestyleModifications: Rest voice and avoid yelling or extended speaking during acute tonsillitis to reduce mucosal stress and pain.
Maintain hydration with cool or warm fluids; ice chips, popsicles, and cold drinks provide topical analgesia.
Use a humidifier at night to prevent throat dryness that worsens discomfort and delays mucosal recovery.
Complete the full 10-day antibiotic course; stopping after 3-4 days of feeling better risks treatment failure and antibiotic resistance.
Monitor for signs of peritonsillar abscess (asymmetric throat swelling, trismus, worsening after initial improvement) and seek urgent care if they appear.
Maintain adequate nutrition — soft, calorie-dense foods (yogurt, smoothies, mashed foods) when swallowing is painful to prevent weight loss, especially in children.

recommended: Cold foods — ice cream, yogurt, frozen fruit bars — provide topical analgesia and are well tolerated when swallowing is painful
Warm broths and soups offer nutrition and hydration with minimal swallowing effort
Soft, non-irritating foods such as porridge, scrambled eggs, bananas, and mashed potato during the acute phase
Honey in warm herbal tea — modest evidence for symptom relief; also adds caloric intake
avoid: Crunchy foods (crackers, crisps, raw vegetables) that scratch inflamed tonsillar tissue
Very spicy or acidic foods that worsen mucosal irritation
Alcohol in adults — dehydrates and impairs local immunity
Carbonated drinks, which can cause irritation and worsen throat discomfort

exerciseGuidelines: Rest during the febrile acute phase. Return to light activity when fever has resolved for 24 hours and swallowing has improved. For infectious mononucleosis, full contact-sport restriction for at least 3 weeks from symptom onset is mandatory due to splenomegaly and splenic rupture risk. Post-tonsillectomy: avoid strenuous exercise and contact sports for 2 weeks; no swimming for 10-14 days.
dailyManagement: Take analgesics on a regular schedule (not just as needed) for the first 3-5 days of acute tonsillitis to maintain comfortable pain control.
Gargle with warm salt water four times daily to reduce mucosal edema and debris in tonsillar crypts.
Monitor temperature once or twice daily; persistent fever above 38.5°C beyond day 3-4 of antibiotics warrants a clinical review.
Check the throat daily for asymmetry, progressive unilateral bulging, or uvular deviation — early signs of peritonsillar abscess.
For children: measure fluid intake and watch for prolonged refusal to swallow even liquids — dehydration risk is high in young children with severe odynophagia.
If taking antibiotics: finish the complete course and do not share antibiotics with family members.
prognosis: Acute tonsillitis carries an excellent prognosis in most patients. Viral tonsillitis resolves spontaneously within 3-7 days. GABHS tonsillitis treated with a complete penicillin course achieves bacteriological cure in 85-90% of patients, with fever resolution within 24-48 hours and full symptom resolution within 5-7 days. Peritonsillar abscess — the most common complication — responds to drainage plus antibiotics in over 90% of cases, with recurrence in approximately 10-20%. Acute rheumatic fever is prevented in over 99% of cases by completing antibiotic therapy. In the absence of treatment, GABHS tonsillitis resolves in 7-14 days in the majority of patients but carries the risks of suppurative spread and rheumatic fever. Children with recurrent tonsillitis who undergo tonsillectomy experience a 50-70% reduction in tonsillitis episodes in the year following surgery. Infectious mononucleosis tonsillitis resolves over 2-6 weeks; profound fatigue persists for months in 10-15% of adolescents.
recoveryTimeline: Viral tonsillitis: peaks at 48-72 hours, resolves in 5-7 days. GABHS with antibiotics: fever resolves in 24-48 hours, swallowing improves in 3-4 days, complete resolution by day 7-10. Post-tonsillectomy recovery: 1-2 weeks for adults, 7-10 days for most children; throat pain often worsens on days 5-7 as scabs separate.
complications: Peritonsillar abscess — the most common complication, affecting 1 in 3,000 episodes; unilateral tonsillar bulge, trismus, uvular deviation, requires drainage plus antibiotics, recurs in 10-20%.

Acute rheumatic fever — follows untreated GABHS tonsillitis in 0.3-3% of endemic-setting cases; causes carditis with potentially permanent mitral or aortic valve damage; prevented by completing antibiotics.

Retropharyngeal or parapharyngeal abscess — deep neck space infection from spread of tonsillar or peritonsillar pus; presents with neck swelling and fever; requires CT diagnosis and surgical drainage.
Airway obstruction — massive bilateral tonsillar enlargement in mononucleosis or rapid-onset bacterial tonsillitis can compromise the oropharyngeal airway, particularly in young children.

Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis — immune-mediated kidney disease 1-3 weeks after GABHS infection; presents with haematuria, proteinuria, and hypertension; not prevented by antibiotics but usually self-limiting.

Lemierre's syndrome — fusobacterial septic thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein following oropharyngeal infection; fever, neck swelling, and septic pulmonary emboli; high mortality without prolonged IV antibiotics.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery: Tonsils and Adenoids
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on tonsillitis, tonsillectomy criteria, recovery, and when to see an ENT specialist.

supportResources.name: CDC — Group A Streptococcal Infections
supportResources.description: CDC guidance on strep tonsillitis diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of spread in schools and households.

supportResources.name: NICE CKS — Tonsillitis
supportResources.description: UK clinical knowledge summary including diagnosis, antibiotic guidance, referral criteria, and tonsillectomy decision support.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Pharyngitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pharyngitis refers to posterior pharyngeal wall inflammation as the dominant finding, whereas tonsillitis centers on tonsillar enlargement, exudate, and crypt inflammation. In practice the two co-exist as tonsillopharyngitis. Tonsillar asymmetry, peritonsillar bulging, or obstructive tonsillar size are features of tonsillitis rather than simple pharyngitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peritonsillar Abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peritonsillar abscess is a complication of tonsillitis with pus accumulation between the tonsil capsule and pharyngeal wall. It presents with unilateral tonsillar bulge pushing the uvula to the opposite side, trismus, and drooling — features absent in uncomplicated tonsillitis. Requires drainage rather than antibiotics alone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epiglottitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Epiglottitis produces severe throat pain and stridor disproportionate to visible tonsillar pathology; in children presents with tripod posture, drooling, and high fever. Tonsillitis shows prominent tonsillar findings on inspection. Throat examination should be deferred if epiglottitis is suspected until airway is secured.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Unilateral tonsillar asymmetry persisting beyond a resolved infection, particularly in adults over 40, raises concern for malignancy rather than recurrent tonsillitis. Nasopharyngeal carcinoma presents with nasal obstruction, epistaxis, and unilateral cervical lymphadenopathy. Biopsy distinguishes it from reactive or infectious tonsillar enlargement.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Pharyngitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Tonsillopharyngitis — simultaneous tonsillar and pharyngeal inflammation — is the most common presentation; the two conditions share pathogens, diagnosis, and treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Peritonsillar Abscess
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common suppurative complication of tonsillitis; prevention by completing antibiotic courses and timely ENT review reduces abscess formation.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Tonsillitis and sinusitis share bacterial and viral pathogens and commonly co-occur during upper respiratory infections, especially in children.

relatedConditions.name: Sleep Apnea (ENT)
relatedConditions.relevance: Tonsillar hypertrophy is a leading cause of pediatric obstructive sleep apnea; tonsillectomy resolves airway obstruction in 70-80% of affected children.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of acute tonsillitis?
faqs.answer: Acute tonsillitis causes sudden sore throat with pain on swallowing, red and swollen tonsils, often with white or yellow patches of pus, fever above 38°C, tender neck glands, bad breath, and sometimes ear pain. Voice may sound muffled. Viral tonsillitis usually also includes a runny nose or cough; bacterial tonsillitis typically does not.

faqs.question: How long does tonsillitis last?
faqs.answer: Viral tonsillitis typically lasts 5-7 days and resolves without treatment. Bacterial tonsillitis (strep) treated with antibiotics improves within 24-48 hours and fully resolves in 7-10 days. Untreated bacterial tonsillitis can persist for 2-3 weeks. Infectious mononucleosis tonsillitis may take 2-4 weeks to resolve, with fatigue lasting months.

faqs.question: Does tonsillitis need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: Not always. Viral tonsillitis — the majority of cases — does not respond to antibiotics and is managed with paracetamol, ibuprofen, and fluids. Antibiotics are needed only for confirmed bacterial tonsillitis (positive rapid strep test or throat culture). Prescribing antibiotics for viral tonsillitis provides no benefit and promotes resistance.

faqs.question: Is tonsillitis contagious?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bacterial tonsillitis (strep) spreads via respiratory droplets and direct contact; patients are contagious until 24 hours after starting antibiotics. Viral tonsillitis from adenovirus or influenza spreads similarly. Infectious mononucleosis (EBV) transmits via saliva and has a longer incubation period of 4-6 weeks.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for tonsillitis?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if sore throat is severe with visible white patches on tonsils, high fever, or tender neck glands; if symptoms persist beyond 5-7 days; if swallowing fluids becomes difficult; or if one side of the throat looks more swollen than the other. Go to the emergency department immediately for drooling, difficulty breathing, or inability to open the mouth fully.

faqs.question: What is a peritonsillar abscess?
faqs.answer: A peritonsillar abscess is a complication of tonsillitis where pus collects between the tonsil and the surrounding muscle. It causes severe one-sided throat pain, difficulty opening the mouth (trismus), drooling, muffled voice, and the affected tonsil bulging toward the midline. It requires emergency drainage by an ENT specialist plus antibiotics.

faqs.question: How many tonsillitis episodes justify tonsillectomy?
faqs.answer: The AAO-HNS Paradise criteria recommend considering tonsillectomy for 7 or more documented episodes in one year, 5 or more per year over two consecutive years, or 3 or more per year over three consecutive years. Each episode must be documented with at least one of: fever, tonsillar exudate, positive strep test, or cervical lymphadenopathy.

faqs.question: What is the recovery time after tonsillectomy?
faqs.answer: Most adults recover in 1-2 weeks; children typically recover in 7-10 days. Throat pain often worsens on days 5-7 as surgical scabs separate. Soft diet for 10-14 days, no strenuous exercise for 2 weeks, and avoidance of aspirin are standard post-operative instructions. Secondary bleeding occurs in 2-3% of patients, usually between days 5-10.

faqs.question: Can tonsillitis cause rheumatic fever?
faqs.answer: Only untreated Group A Strep tonsillitis causes acute rheumatic fever — viral tonsillitis does not. Rheumatic fever follows GABHS infection in 0.3-3% of untreated cases and can cause permanent heart valve damage. Completing the full 10-day antibiotic course eliminates this risk in over 99% of cases.

faqs.question: Can tonsillitis cause ear pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Referred ear pain (otalgia) is common in tonsillitis, experienced by about 50% of patients. It is transmitted through the glossopharyngeal nerve, which supplies both the tonsil and the ear. The ear itself is not infected; the pain resolves as tonsillitis improves. Ear pain during tonsillitis does not require separate ear treatment.

faqs.question: What is the difference between tonsillitis and glandular fever?
faqs.answer: Tonsillitis from bacteria (strep) or adenovirus causes sore throat and tonsil inflammation. Glandular fever (infectious mononucleosis from EBV) causes the same but with extreme fatigue, posterior neck glands swollen, possible spleen enlargement, and a characteristic rash if given amoxicillin. Glandular fever is confirmed by a monospot blood test and does not respond to antibiotics.

faqs.question: What home remedies help tonsillitis?
faqs.answer: Ibuprofen (anti-inflammatory) or paracetamol for pain and fever, warm salt water gargles four times daily, warm or cold fluids, honey in herbal tea, throat lozenges, and a humidifier at night all help manage symptoms. Home remedies do not cure bacterial tonsillitis — antibiotic therapy is needed for confirmed strep infections.

faqs.question: Can tonsillitis recur after tonsillectomy?
faqs.answer: True tonsillitis cannot recur after tonsillectomy because the tonsils are removed. However, residual tonsillar tissue occasionally persists if removal was incomplete, and pharyngitis from the remaining posterior pharyngeal lymphoid tissue can still occur. Recurrent episodes resemble mild sore throats rather than severe tonsillitis. Tonsillectomy reduces sore throat episodes by 50-70% in the year after surgery.

faqs.question: Can adults get tonsillitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Tonsillitis affects adults as well as children, though it is less frequent in adults. Group A Strep accounts for 5-15% of adult sore throats. Adults experience the same complications as children, including peritonsillar abscess, which actually peaks in young adults. The tonsils do not shrink after adolescence in all people.

faqs.question: Is white spots on tonsils always tonsillitis?
faqs.answer: Not always. White spots can result from bacterial tonsillitis (strep), viral tonsillitis (adenovirus, EBV mononucleosis), tonsil stones (calcified debris in tonsillar crypts), oral thrush (Candida), or aphthous ulcers. A rapid strep test and clinical examination distinguish bacterial tonsillitis from these other causes.
